en.extract:
The Rarotonga monarch (Pomarea dimidiata), also known as the Rarotonga flycatcher or kākerōri, is a species of bird in the monarch flycatcher family Monarchidae. It is endemic to Rarotonga in the Cook Islands, and has been introduced to Atiu, also in the Cook Islands.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Rarotonga monarch was originally described in the genus Monarcha. Alternate names include Cook Island flycatcher, Cook Islands monarch, kakerori, and Rarotonga monarch-flycatcher.


Description
The Rarotonga monarch is an insectivorous passerine which is 10 cm tall, with an adult mass of 20 grams (0.7 oz) for female birds, and 25 grams (0.9 oz) for large male birds. Hunting mainly consists of foraging for insects within foliage, and occasionally taking insects during flight.

The Rarotonga monarch is unusual as its plumage and beak undergoes a sequential colour change as the bird matures. The initial plumage of orange changes to orange-grey mixed, then finally grey when maturity is reached, after four years. Initially, the grey and orange birds were attributed to different species. Later, it was thought that grey plumage birds were male, while the orange birds were females or juveniles of the same species, but this was corrected to being age-based plumage in 1983. Another bird in the Pomarea genus, the Fatu Hiva monarch, also undergoes a sequential colour change as it ages.


Behaviour and ecology
Owing to its tropical oceanic island location, the Rarotonga monarch is exceptionally long-lived, with an adult survival of between 85 and 89 percent, a life expectancy of seven to nine years, and a maximum known lifespan of around 24 years and 8 months. These figures are comparable to large Australian passerines like the superb lyrebird or satin bowerbird and more than ten times the life expectancies of similar sized Holarctic songbirds.


Breeding
The extraordinary longevity of these birds may explain the evolution of helpers at the nest, a feature otherwise completely absent from the Monarchidae family. Males can breed at one year, but do not do so in practice until they are four years old. Breeding season is between October and February, but most eggs are laid between October and early November. Nests are mossy deep cups which are built in the fork of a tree branch, often constructed above creeks, and take about 7 days to build. Incubation last 13–14 days. Clutches are 1–2 eggs. In Rarotonga, many nest failures are attributed to predation by black rats or kiore, as birds sit on the nest.


Threats
Since the introduction of the black rat and feral cat, adult mortality has more than doubled, a change sufficient to reduce what was previously a very numerous bird to one of the most endangered birds in the world by the middle 1980s, when the Rarotonga monarch was listed as one of the highest conservation priorities among all Pacific Island birds. The annual pre-breeding removal of rats (starting in the late 1980s) from its principal breeding area on the south coast of Rarotonga (at the Takitumu Conservation Area) by staff and volunteers has made breeding significantly more successful: around two thirds of pairs assisted by a few helpers can now rear the normal clutch of two eggs, whereas in the 1980s breeding attempts had a success rate as low as eleven percent. Despite the growth in population, a major tropical cyclone could destroy this population growth with extreme swiftness, so that conservation work is still very important.


Status
In the 1989, the total population was estimated at fewer than 29 birds in the wild, and was critically endangered but has recovered since annual rat bait laying during the breeding season. The Rarotonga monarch was limited to Rarotonga until a second population was established on Atiu Island⁣ by translocating 30 young birds in 2001, to establish an 'insurance population', should the Rarotongan bird population suffer a catastrophe.
In 2022 the total species population was assessed at 500 mature birds, and is now regarded as vulnerable. This reversal in conservation status is regarded as a major success story for South Pacific bird conservation efforts, with the Takitumu Conservation Area project receiving a Conversation Award by Birdlife International in 2022.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.
Kākerōri Conservation Project Bird Biology Page
Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Birds of the World: Rarotonga Monarch

The '''Rarotonga monarch''' ('''''Pomarea dimidiata'''''), also known as the '''Rarotonga flycatcher''' or '''kākerōri,''' is a species of [[bird]] in the monarch flycatcher family [[Monarchidae]].<ref>{{cite web |url= http://cookislands.bishopmuseum.org/species.asp?id=8691 |title= Rarotonga Flycatcher |work= [[Cook Islands Biodiversity|Cook Islands Biodiversity & Natural Heritage]] |access-date=7 March 2013}}</ref> It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Rarotonga]] in the Cook Islands, and has been [[Introduced species|introduced]] to [[Atiu]], also in the Cook Islands.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The Rarotonga monarch was originally described in the genus ''[[Monarcha]]''. Alternate names include '''Cook Island flycatcher''', '''Cook Islands monarch''', '''kakerori''', and '''Rarotonga monarch-flycatcher'''.

==Description==
The Rarotonga monarch is an [[insectivorous]] [[passerine]] which is 10 cm tall, with an adult mass of {{convert|20|g|oz|1}} for female birds, and {{convert|25|g|oz|1}} for large male birds.<ref name=":0">{{Cite thesis |title=The Ecology of the Kākerōri (Rarotonga Flycatcher) ''Pomarea dimidiata'', With Special Reference to Fledged Young |url= https://mro.massey.ac.nz/handle/10179/12297 |publisher=Massey University |date=1993 |degree=MSc |first=Kerry H. |last=Sanders}}</ref> Hunting mainly consists of foraging for insects within foliage, and occasionally taking insects during flight.<ref name=":0" />

[[File:Kakerori Plumage Aging.jpg|alt=|left|thumb|309x309px|'''<big>A</big>''': Orange with yellow in the beak the near head, correlates to less than a year old. '''<big>B:</big>''' Orange with Blue/Black beak, correlates to an age of two years.

'''<big>C:</big>''' Mixed plumage, with orange wing feathers still present, but with some grey plumage, correlating to about three years old. '''<big>D:</big>''' Grey plumage, correlates with an age of four years and older. This is the final plumage stage for the remainder of the bird's lifespan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Age and sex determination of Kakerori Pomarea dimidiata |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/age-and-sex-determination-of-kakerori-pomarea-dimidiata/ |access-date=2023-09-03 |website=Birds New Zealand |language=en-NZ}}</ref>]]

The Rarotonga monarch is unusual as its plumage and beak undergoes a sequential colour change as the bird matures. The initial plumage of orange changes to orange-grey mixed, then finally grey when maturity is reached, after four years.<ref name=":2" /> Initially, the grey and orange birds were attributed to different species.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Gill |first=William Wyatt |title=Jottings from the Pacific |publisher=Religious Tract Society |year=1885 |pages=127–128 |language=English}}</ref> Later, it was thought that grey plumage birds were male, while the orange birds were females or juveniles of the same species, but this was corrected to being age-based plumage in 1983.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="gov">[http://www.doc.govt.nz/upload/documents/science-and-technical/drds246.pdf "Conservation of kakerori (''Pomarea dimidiata'') in the Cook Islands in 2004–05"]</ref> Another bird in the ''[[Pomarea]]'' genus, the [[Fatu Hiva monarch]], also undergoes a sequential colour change as it ages.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-03-28 |title=Saving the Fatu Hiva monarch from the brink of extinction |url=https://www.aucklandzoo.co.nz/saving-the-fatu-hiva-monarch-from-the-brink-of-extinction |access-date=2024-12-03 |website=www.aucklandzoo.co.nz |language=en}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
Owing to its tropical oceanic island location, the Rarotonga monarch is exceptionally long-lived,<ref name="gov"/> with an adult survival of between 85 and 89 percent, a life expectancy of seven to nine years, and a maximum known lifespan of around 24 years and 8 months.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Biology – Kakerori Bird {{!}} Cook Islands |url=https://www.kakerori.com/biology/ |access-date=2023-09-16 |language=en-US}}</ref> These figures are comparable to large Australian passerines like the [[superb lyrebird]] or [[satin bowerbird]]<ref>See [http://www.environment.gov.au/biodiversity/science/abbbs/abbbs-search.html Australian Bird and Bat Banding Scheme]</ref> and more than ten times the life expectancies of similar sized [[Holarctic]] songbirds.

===Breeding===
The extraordinary longevity of these birds may explain the evolution of [[helpers at the nest]], a feature otherwise completely absent from the Monarchidae family.<ref>[http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/suppl/2009/02/12/273.1592.1375.DC1/PB061375Supp2.pdf Distribution of known and inferred modes of parental care in families and subfamilies of birds]</ref> Males can breed at one year, but do not do so in practice until they are four years old.<ref name="gov1">{{Cite journal |title=Breeding biology of the Kakerori (''Pomarea dimidiata'') on Rarotonga, Cook Islands |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/breeding-biology-of-the-kakerori-pomarea-dimidiata-on-rarotonga-cook-islands/ |date=1998 |journal=Notornis |volume=45 |access-date=2023-09-16 |via=Birds New Zealand}}</ref> Breeding season is between October and February, but most eggs are laid between October and early November.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Biology – Kakerori Bird {{!}} Cook Islands |url=https://www.kakerori.com/biology/ |access-date=2023-09-03 |language=en-US}}</ref> Nests are mossy deep cups which are built in the fork of a tree branch, often constructed above creeks, and take about 7 days to build.<ref name=":0" /> Incubation last 13–14 days.<ref name=":0" /> Clutches are 1–2 eggs. In Rarotonga, many nest failures are attributed to predation by [[black rat]]s or [[Polynesian rat|kiore]], as birds sit on the nest.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |url=https://chm.cbd.int/api/v2013/documents/B63E7679-3021-D4C6-9E51-67575CF0AB8E/attachments/213255/Takitumu%20Conservation%20Area%20Management%20Plan%202020%E2%80%932030.pdf |title=Takitumu Conservation Area Management Plan 2020–2030 |pages=38, 23–24 |language=English}}</ref>

===Threats===
Since the introduction of the black rat and [[feral cat]], adult mortality has more than doubled, a change sufficient to reduce what was previously a very numerous bird<ref name="gov1"/> to one of the most endangered birds in the world by the middle 1980s, when the Rarotonga monarch was listed as one of the highest conservation priorities among all Pacific Island birds.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Sherley |first=Greg |title=Bird Conservation Priorities and a draft [avifauna] Conservation Strategy for the Pacific Islands region / compiled by Greg Sherley |url=https://library.sprep.org/content/bird-conservation-priorities-and-draft-avifauna-conservation-strategy-pacific-islands |access-date=2023-09-16 |website=library.sprep.org |language=en}}</ref> The annual pre-breeding removal of rats (starting in the late 1980s) from its principal breeding area on the south coast of Rarotonga (at the [https://tiscookislands.org/the-takitumu-conservation-area/ Takitumu Conservation Area]) by staff and volunteers has made breeding significantly more successful: around two thirds of pairs assisted by a few helpers can now rear the normal clutch of two eggs, whereas in the 1980s breeding attempts had a success rate as low as eleven percent.<ref name="gov1"/> Despite the growth in population, a major [[tropical cyclone]] could destroy this population growth with extreme swiftness, so that conservation work is still very important.<ref name=":1" />

==Status==
In the 1989, the total population was estimated at fewer than 29 birds in the wild, and was [[Critically Endangered|critically endangered]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=Department of Conservation |first= |date=2022-07-22 |title=Hugh helping to bring back the kākerōri {{!}} Conservation blog |url=https://blog.doc.govt.nz/2022/07/23/bringing-back-the-kakerori/,%20https://blog.doc.govt.nz/2022/07/23/bringing-back-the-kakerori/ |access-date=2023-09-16 |website=blog.doc.govt.nz |language=en-US}}</ref> but has recovered since annual [[Rodenticide|rat bait laying]] during the breeding season.<ref name=CIN2022a>{{cite web |url=https://www.cookislandsnews.com/uncategorised/national/local/environment/kakerori-revitalisation-project-wins-major-conservation-award/ |title=Kākerōri revitalisation project wins major conservation award |publisher=Cook Islands News |author=Matthew Littlewood |date=22 September 2022 |access-date=23 September 2022}}</ref> The Rarotonga monarch was limited to Rarotonga until a second population was established on [[Atiu Island|Atiu Island⁣]] by [[Translocation (Wildlife conservation)|translocating]] 30 young birds in 2001, to establish an 'insurance population', should the Rarotongan bird population suffer a catastrophe.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Robertson |first=Hugh A. |last2=Karika |first2=Ian |last3=Saul |first3=Edward K. |date=2006 |title=Translocation of Rarotonga Monarchs Pomarea dimidiata within the southern Cook Islands |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bird-conservation-international/article/translocation-of-rarotonga-monarchs-pomarea-dimidiata-within-the-southern-cook-islands/8DA899016CDE192699CCF045FA0B9DF3 |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=197–215 |doi=10.1017/S0959270906000268 |issn=1474-0001|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |author=Peter de Graaf |date=4 November 2018 |title=Cook Islands: The Birdman of Atiu |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/travel/cook-islands-the-birdman-of-atiu/C5KLXKPUCSCWZBYMJ63J333ECY/ |access-date=8 February 2021 |publisher=New Zealand Herald}}</ref>

In 2022 the total species population was assessed at 500 mature birds, and is now regarded as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Rarotonga Monarch (Pomarea dimidiata) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/rarotonga-monarch-pomarea-dimidiata |access-date=2023-09-16 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref> This reversal in conservation status is regarded as a major success story for South Pacific bird conservation efforts, with the Takitumu Conservation Area project receiving a Conversation Award by Birdlife International in 2022.<ref name="CIN2022a" />
